moveAnnotations.annotation: This is the King's Indian Defense, one of the most dynamic and aggressive openings for Black. White plays 1.d4, claiming the center. The KID leads to sharp, double-edged positions where Black allows White to build a full center, then counter-attacks with ...e5 or ...f5 — creating some of chess's most exciting middlegames.
plans: Build a broad center with c4 and e4
alternatives: e4 -- leads to different openings entirely

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays 1...Nf6, the Indian Defense approach — contesting central control with pieces before committing pawns. The Nf6 controls e4 and d5, preventing White from achieving an easy e4 without preparation.
plans: Play ...g6 and ...Bg7 for the King's Indian setup
Let White build the center, then attack it
alternatives: d5 -- the classical approach, meeting the center head-on

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays c4, expanding central control alongside d4. The c4+d4 pawn duo controls d5, e5, c5, and b5 — a formidable central presence that Black will challenge from the flanks.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's c4+d4 broad center is the foundation of their strategy
plans: Play Nc3 and e4 for maximum central control

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays g6, the King's Indian setup. The g6 pawn prepares the fianchetto of the dark-squared bishop to g7, where it will become Black's most powerful piece — controlling the long a1-h8 diagonal and supporting the eventual ...e5 central break.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's kingside fianchetto with g6 creates a fortress but gives White central space
plans: Fianchetto with Bg7, castle, then strike with ...e5 or ...c5
alternatives: d5 -- the Grunfeld, challenging the center immediately with the bishop alread...

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops Nc3, reinforcing the center and controlling d5. The Nc3 supports the e4 advance that creates White's ideal center. The KID allows White this full setup — the fight comes later.
plans: Play e4 to build the full center
The knight supports d5 and e4

moveAnnotations.annotation: The bishop takes its throne on g7, the heart of the King's Indian. From g7, the bishop controls the long a1-h8 diagonal, defends the kingside dark squares, and will become enormously powerful once Black plays ...e5 and the center opens.
plans: The Bg7 pressures d4 and supports a future ...e5 break
Castle next to complete kingside development
alternatives: Already committed to the KID setup -- this is the only consistent move

moveAnnotations.annotation: White pushes e4, establishing the full d4+e4 center — the broadest possible pawn formation. This is exactly what the KID allows: White gets maximum central space, but Black's hypermodern strategy bets that this center can be attacked and undermined.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's c4-d4-e4 pawn center is the maximum possible central presence
plans: Use the space advantage for piece development and queenside expansion
alternatives: This is the main line -- any other move is less ambitious

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays d6, supporting a future ...e5 central break and creating the classic KID pawn structure. The d6 pawn is defensive but essential — it holds the center together and prevents e5 from White.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Black's d6 pawn supports the upcoming ...e5 central challenge
plans: Play ...e5 to challenge White's center
Castle and complete the KID setup
alternatives: O-O -- castling first is also possible, but d6 is needed before ...e5

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the knight to f3, completing kingside piece development and supporting the d4 and e4 pawns. The Nf3 controls e5, prepares castling, and adds another defender to the center.
plans: Castle and maintain the center
After ...e5, decide between dxe5, d5, or maintaining tension
alternatives: Be2 -- developing the bishop first, also standard

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black castles kingside, getting the king safe behind the fianchettoed Bg7. In the KID, early castling is essential — the kingside pawn storm (...f5-f4) comes later, and the king must be safe first.
plans: Play ...e5, then ...f5 for a kingside attack
The Rf8 supports the ...f5 break

moveAnnotations.annotation: White develops the bishop to e2, preparing to castle. The Be2 is the Classical KID choice — solid and flexible, it supports both castling and a future f3 to reinforce e4. The alternative Bd3 is more aggressive but can be hit by ...Nc5.
plans: Castle next, then decide on a plan based on Black's setup
alternatives: Bd3 -- more active but blocks the d-file in the main line structures

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black plays e5, the signature King's Indian central break. This bold advance challenges White's d4 pawn directly, stakes Black's claim in the center, and opens lines for the Bg7 and the Rf8. After d5, the position closes and the classic KID race begins.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: After d5, a locked center creates a classic KID pawn structure with opposite-flank attacks
plans: After d5, launch a kingside attack with ...f5, ...Nf6-h5-f4
The Bg7 comes alive pressuring d4 from behind
alternatives: Nc6 -- developing first before ...e5, but the immediate break is the main line

moveAnnotations.annotation: White castles, getting the king to safety before the center closes. After the eventual d5 closing the center, both sides will attack on opposite flanks — White on the queenside with c5-c6, Black on the kingside with f5-f4-g5.
plans: Play d5 to lock the center, then expand on the queenside with c5, a4, b4
alternatives: d5 -- closing the center immediately is also played

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black develops the knight to c6, pressuring d4 and preparing to support the ...e5 pawn. The Nc6 also eyes d4 and b4, and after d5 closes the center, the knight will retreat to e7 or a5 for repositioning.
plans: Force d5, then reroute the knight via e7 to g6 or f5 for the kingside attack
alternatives: Nbd7 -- keeping the knight out of the way of d5, but Nc6 forces the issue mor...

moveAnnotations.annotation: White pushes d5, closing the center. This is the defining moment of the KID — the locked center creates a classic race. White will attack on the queenside with c5-c6-b4, while Black storms the kingside with ...f5-f4-g5-g4. The side that breaks through first usually wins.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: Locked center: White's c4-d5-e4 chain vs Black's d6-e5. Opposite-side attacks begin.
plans: Expand on the queenside with c5, a4, b4
Win the race against Black's kingside attack
alternatives: dxe5 -- opening the center is possible but gives Black excellent piece play w...

moveAnnotations.annotation: The knight retreats to e7 after d5 blocks its path on c6. From e7, the knight reroutes to g6 (supporting the ...f5 kingside attack), d5 (if the c6 route is available), or f5 (a powerful outpost in some lines).
plans: Reroute to g6, f5, or d7 depending on the position
Support ...f5 from e7
alternatives: Na5 -- attacking c4 immediately, a sideline but less common

moveAnnotations.annotation: White retreats the knight to e1, preparing the f3 reinforcement of e4 and freeing the Nf3 square for other pieces. The Ne1 will reroute to d3 where it supports c5 and eyes e5 and f4 — key squares in the queenside attack.
plans: Reroute Nd3, supporting c5 and controlling f4
Clear f3 for the f-pawn if needed
alternatives: Nd2 -- another regrouping option, heading for c5 via b3

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black repositions the knight to d7, preparing to support the ...f5 kingside break. The Nd7 can jump to f8-h7 (supporting ...g5-g4), c5 (hitting d3 and e4), or b6 (pressuring d5 and c4).
plans: Clear f6 for the ...f5 break
Reroute Nd7-f6 or Nd7-c5 depending on the position
alternatives: f5 -- playing the break immediately is possible, but Nd7 first improves the k...

moveAnnotations.annotation: White plays f3, reinforcing the e4 pawn and creating a solid f3-e4 chain. The f3 move also frees the queen from e4 defense duties and prepares potential g4 ideas to prevent Black's ...f5 advance.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: White's f3 creates an ironclad e4 support but commits to a closed kingside structure
plans: Support e4 permanently, prepare queenside expansion with b4 and c5
Consider g4 to prevent ...f5
alternatives: Nd3 -- continuing the knight rerouting, also standard

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black strikes with f5, the signature King's Indian break that the entire setup was designed for. The f5 pawn attacks e4, opens the f-file for the rook, and after exf5 gxf5, the g-file becomes a devastating attacking highway aimed at White's king.
moveAnnotations.pawnStructure: ...f5 creates the classic KID attack structure; after ...f4 Black gets a kingside pawn storm
plans: Play ...f4 to seal the kingside, then ...g5, ...h5, ...Rf6-g6 for a mating at...
If exf5, recapture ...gxf5 and use the open g-file
alternatives: a5 -- preventing White's b4, a slower approach but sometimes necessary

moveAnnotations.annotation: Black moves the bishop to d7. The bishop on d7 controls key diagonal squares and maintains active piece play.
The Petrosian System (d5 without e4) is an important variation of the King's Indian Defence. The Petrosian system with d5 and Bg5 tries to restrict Black's kingside play.
Black replies with Nf6. This is the defining move of the Petrosian System (d5 without e4) — it shapes the pawn structure and determines the resulting plans.
g6 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
Bg7 — Black fianchettoes the bishop, placing it on the long diagonal where it controls the center from a distance. This is a key positional idea.
e4 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
e5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
d5 stakes a claim in the center. Central pawns control space and restrict the opponent's piece activity.
h6 pushes on the kingside. This pawn advance gains space and can support a future attack toward the enemy king.
Bh4 improves the bishop's placement. The bishop was less effective on g5 and moves to h4 where it serves the plan better.
Nd2 improves the knight's placement. The knight was less effective on f3 and moves to d2 where it serves the plan better.
Qe8 activates the queen. From e8, the queen has maximum influence across multiple diagonals and files. We're reaching the critical position of this variation.
Nh7. This is the typical position arising from the Petrosian System (d5 without e4). From here, understanding the strategic plans — piece placement, pawn breaks, and targets — is essential.
In the King's Indian Defence, the Kingside Pawn Storm is a tactical pattern you should know. When White tries to stop ...f5 with g4, Black plays ...Kh8 and ...fxg4, opening the f-file.
In the King's Indian Defence, the Central Exchange Tactic is a tactical pattern you should know. If White captures on e5 in the exchange variation, Black gets active piece play.
In the King's Indian Defence, the Ignoring the Queenside is a dangerous line you must be aware of.
cxd6 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
cxd6 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
In the King's Indian Defence, the Premature ...f5 Without Ne7 is a dangerous line you must be aware of.
gxf5 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
gxf5 — this capture changes the character of the position. Be alert.
White opens with 1.d4, the starting point for the King's Indian Defense. Black will allow White to build a broad center and then counterattack it with ...e5 or ...c5.
Black develops the knight to f6, the signature first move of the King's Indian. The knight controls e4 and d5 while Black waits to see White's setup before committing pawns.
White plays c4, establishing the classical pawn duo. Together with d4, this controls the center and prepares Nc3 to reinforce the pawn structure.
Black plays ...g6, entering the King's Indian setup. The fianchettoed bishop on g7 will be Black's most important piece, pressuring the center along the long diagonal.
White develops the knight to c3, reinforcing control of d5 and e4. This is the most active move, preparing to build the full classical center with e4.
Black completes the fianchetto, placing the bishop on the powerful long diagonal. The g7 bishop is the soul of Black's position in the KID, exerting constant pressure toward the center and queenside.
White plays e4, establishing the imposing classical center with pawns on c4, d4, and e4.
Black plays ...d6, supporting a future ...e5 advance. The pawn on d6 is a hallmark of the KID — it holds the center together and prepares the kingside counterattack.
White develops the last minor piece, completing the Classical setup. The knight on f3 supports d4 and e5, and White is ready to castle and enter the middlegame.
Black castles kingside, securing the king before the central clash. The king is safe behind the fianchettoed bishop, and the f8 rook will support the ...f5 pawn break.
White develops the bishop to e2, a modest but solid square. The bishop supports castling and keeps options open — it can later move to f3 or d3 depending on the position.
Black plays the thematic ...e5, directly challenging White's center. This is the defining move of the Classical KID — Black fights for space in the center and prepares the kingside pawn storm with .
White castles, getting the king to safety before the center closes. With ...e5 played, the center will likely lock after d5, and both sides will attack on opposite flanks.
Black develops the knight to c6, increasing pressure on d4. This is the Classical main line move — the knight supports ...e5 and forces White to make a decision about the center.
White closes the center with d5, the critical moment of the Classical KID. This locks the pawn structure and commits both sides to opposite-flank attacks — White pushes on the queenside (c5, b4), Bl.
The knight retreats to e7, heading for the kingside. From e7 it supports ...f5 and can reroute to g6 or f5 to join the attack. This is the standard regrouping in the Classical KID.
White plays Ne1, the first step of the signature knight maneuver in this system. The knight leaves f3 to reroute to d3, where it will control c5 and support the b4 queenside advance.
Black repositions the knight from f6 to d7, clearing the f-file for the ...f5 pawn break. The knight on d7 can later go to f6, c5, or f8 depending on how the position develops.
White completes the Ne1-d3 maneuver, placing the knight on an excellent square. From d3, it controls c5 (preventing Black's counterplay there), supports b4, and keeps an eye on f4 to slow Black's ki.
Black launches the kingside attack with ...f5, the signature KID break. This challenges White's e4 pawn and opens lines for the rooks.
White plays f3, bolstering the e4 pawn and keeping the center closed. This is a critical defensive resource — without f3, Black's ...f4 would rip open the kingside too easily.
Black plays ...f4, locking the kingside pawn structure and preparing the pawn storm with ...g5-g4. This closes the center completely, giving Black a free hand on the kingside.
